Now I have a much clearer picture. Let me organize the nodes:
The rotating frame has blocks arranged in a rectangular pattern. Looking at the nodes:
- Top row (rows 19-22): w, c, g, S, b, p, Y, g (8 blocks, each 4x4)
- Middle row (rows 25-28): S (left), p (right)
- Bottom row (rows 31-34): p (left), b (right)
- Lower row (rows 37-40): g (left), S (right)
- Bottom row (rows 43-46): w, w, b, b, S, p, g (7 blocks)

- id=14: w, 16 pixels, [[19, 12], [19, 15], [22, 15], [22, 12]] - 4x4 block at rows 19-22, cols 12-15
- id=15: c, 8 pixels, [[19, 16], [19, 17], [22, 17], [22, 16]] - 4x2 block at rows 19-22, cols 16-17
- id=16: g, 16 pixels, [[19, 18], [19, 21], [22, 21], [22, 18]] - 4x4 block at rows 19-22, cols 18-21
- id=17: S, 16 pixels, [[19, 24], [19, 27], [22, 27], [22, 24]] - 4x4 block at rows 19-22, cols 24-27
- id=18: b, 16 pixels, [[19, 30], [19, 33], [22, 33], [22, 30]] - 4x4 block at rows 19-22, cols 30-33
- id=19: p, 16 pixels, [[19, 36], [19, 39], [22, 39], [22, 36]] - 4x4 block at rows 19-22, cols 36-39
- id=20: Y, 16 pixels, [[19, 42], [19, 45], [22, 45], [22, 42]] - 4x4 block at rows 19-22, cols 42-45
- id=21: g, 16 pixels, [[19, 48], [19, 51], [22, 51], [22, 48]] - 4x4 block at rows 19-22, cols 48-51
So the top row has 8 blocks: w, c, g, S, b, p, Y, g
Wait, but id=15 is c with 8 pixels (4x2), not 16 pixels (4x4). So it's a different size. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the ASCII:
Row 19: NcccccccccccwwwwccggggccSSSSccbbbbccppppccYYYYccgggg
So the pattern is: N (left bar), c (charcoal background), wwww (light gray block), cc (charcoal), gggg (gray block), cc, SSSS (sky blue), cc, bbbb (blue), cc, pppp (purple), cc, YYYY (yellow), cc, gggg (gray)
So the top row has blocks: w, g, S, b, p, Y, g (7 blocks, each 4x4)
Wait, but id=14 is w at cols 12-15 (4 cols), id=15 is c at cols 16-17 (2 cols), id=16 is g at cols 18-21 (4 cols), etc. So the blocks are separated by 2-col gaps of charcoal.
Actually, looking more carefully, id=15 is c (charcoal) with 8 pixels at [[19, 16], [19, 17], [22, 17], [22, 16]]. This is a 4x2 block of charcoal. But the background is also charcoal (c). So this is just part of the background, not a separate block.
Wait, but the segmentation treats it as a separate node. This is because the charcoal blocks are enclosed by the charcoal background but are separate connected components.
OK, let me focus on the colored blocks (not charcoal):
- id=14: w (light gray), 16 pixels, rows 19-22, cols 12-15
- id=16: g (gray), 16 pixels, rows 19-22, cols 18-21
- id=17: S (sky blue), 16 pixels, rows 19-22, cols 24-27
- id=18: b (blue), 16 pixels, rows 19-22, cols 30-33
- id=19: p (purple), 16 pixels, rows 19-22, cols 36-39
- id=20: Y (yellow), 16 pixels, rows 19-22, cols 42-45
- id=21: g (gray), 16 pixels, rows 19-22, cols 48-51
So the top row has 7 colored blocks: w, g, S, b, p, Y, g
Now let me look at the other rows:
- id=25: S, 16 pixels, rows 25-28, cols 12-15 (left side)
- id=26: p, 16 pixels, rows 25-28, cols 48-51 (right side)
- id=29: p, 16 pixels, rows 31-34, cols 12-15 (left side)
- id=30: b, 16 pixels, rows 31-34, cols 48-51 (right side)
- id=31: g, 16 pixels, rows 37-40, cols 12-15 (left side)
- id=32: S, 16 pixels, rows 37-40, cols 48-51 (right side)
- id=33: w, 16 pixels, rows 43-46, cols 12-15
- id=34: w, 16 pixels, rows 43-46, cols 18-21
- id=35: b, 16 pixels, rows 43-46, cols 24-27
- id=36: b, 16 pixels, rows 43-46, cols 30-33
- id=37: S, 16 pixels, rows 43-46, cols 36-39
- id=38: p, 16 pixels, rows 43-46, cols 42-45
- id=39: g, 16 pixels, rows 43-46, cols 48-51
So the bottom row has 7 colored blocks: w, w, b, b, S, p, g
And the middle rows have:
- Row 25-28: S (left), p (right)
- Row 31-34: p (left), b (right)
- Row 37-40: g (left), S (right)
So the rotating frame is a rectangular arrangement of colored blocks. The blocks on the left and right sides are in the middle rows, and the blocks on the top and bottom are in the top and bottom rows.
Now, the fixed blocks are:
- id=12: Y, 4 pixels, rows 17-18, cols 10-11 (above the frame)
- id=13: Y, 4 pixels, rows 17-18, cols 16-17 (above the frame)
- id=22: Y, 4 pixels, rows 23-24, cols 10-11 (left of the frame)
- id=23: c, 8 pixels, rows 23-24, cols 12-15 (left of the frame, but this is charcoal)
- id=24: Y, 4 pixels, rows 23-24, cols 16-17 (left of the frame)
Wait, id=23 is c (charcoal) with 8 pixels at [[23, 12], [23, 15], [24, 15], [24, 12]]. This is a 2x4 block of charcoal. But the background is also charcoal. So this is just part of the background.
Actually, looking at the ASCII:
Row 23: NcccccccccYYccccYYcccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccc
So the pattern is: N (left bar), c (charcoal background), YY (yellow block), cccc (charcoal), YY (yellow block), c (charcoal background)
So the fixed yellow blocks are at:
- (17-18, 10-11): Y
- (17-18, 16-17): Y
- (23-24, 10-11): Y
- (23-24, 16-17): Y
These form a 2x2 pattern of yellow blocks.
Now, the rotating blocks are in a frame. The frame has:
- Top row (rows 19-22): w, g, S, b, p, Y, g (7 blocks)
- Middle rows (rows 25-40): left side has S, p, g; right side has p, b, S
- Bottom row (rows 43-46): w, w, b, b, S, p, g (7 blocks)
So the rotating frame is a rectangular arrangement of colored blocks. The blocks rotate when clicking the red or green shapes.
Now, the goal is probably to rotate the colors to match some target configuration. The target configuration might be indicated by the fixed yellow blocks.
But I've tried all 20 rotations and none of them completed the level. So the goal is not about reaching a specific rotation.
Wait, maybe I need to look at this differently. The fixed yellow blocks form a 2x2 pattern. Maybe the goal is to make the rotating blocks form a similar pattern.
Actually, let me think about the structure of the rotating frame. The frame has:
- Top row: 7 blocks
- Middle rows: 3 blocks on left, 3 blocks on right
- Bottom row: 7 blocks
This forms a rectangular frame with blocks on the perimeter. The blocks rotate around the perimeter.
Let me count the total number of blocks:
- Top row: 7 blocks
- Left side (middle): 3 blocks
- Right side (middle): 3 blocks
- Bottom row: 7 blocks
Total: 7 + 3 + 3 + 7 = 20 blocks
So there are 20 blocks in the rotating frame. This matches the 20 rotations I tried (clicking red 20 times returns to the original configuration).
Now, the goal is probably to rotate the colors to a specific configuration. But I've tried all 20 rotations and none of them completed the level.
Wait, maybe the goal is not about rotating the colors at all. Maybe the goal is about something else entirely.
Let me think about the fixed yellow blocks. They form a 2x2 pattern at (17-18, 10-11), (17-18, 16-17), (23-24, 10-11), (23-24, 16-17). This pattern is above and to the left of the rotating frame.
Maybe the goal is to match the colors of the rotating blocks to the fixed yellow blocks. But the fixed yellow blocks are all yellow, so this doesn't make sense.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the fixed blocks. The fixed blocks are:
- id=12: Y, 4 pixels, rows 17-18, cols 10-11
- id=13: Y, 4 pixels, rows 17-18, cols 16-17
- id=22: Y, 4 pixels, rows 23-24, cols 10-11
- id=24: Y, 4 pixels, rows 23-24, cols 16-17
These are all yellow blocks. But there's also:
- id=23: c, 8 pixels, rows 23-24, cols 12-15
This is a charcoal block between the yellow blocks at rows 23-24. So the pattern is:
- Row 17-18: Y, c, Y (yellow, charcoal, yellow)
- Row 23-24: Y, c, Y (yellow, charcoal, yellow)
Wait, but id=23 is c (charcoal) with 8 pixels at [[23, 12], [23, 15], [24, 15], [24, 12]]. This is a 2x4 block of charcoal. So the pattern is:
- Row 17-18: Y (2x2), c (2x2), Y (2x2)
- Row 23-24: Y (2x2), c (2x4), Y (2x2)
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just try to understand the goal by looking at the initial state and the current state.
Actually, I just realized something. The game says the HUD is `rows 19-46, cols 0-51`. This is the area where the rotating blocks are. The game considers this area as the HUD because it changes predictably with each action.
But the game also says `gameplay_changed: False` when I click on the red or green shapes. This means the game doesn't consider the color rotation as a gameplay change.
So the game thinks the rotating blocks are the HUD, and clicking on the red/green shapes only changes the HUD, not the gameplay.
But the only interaction is rotating the colors. So either:
1. The goal is to rotate the colors to a specific configuration (but I've tried all 20 rotations)
2. The goal is something else entirely, and I haven't found the right interaction yet
Let me try clicking on the black blocks at the top (the timer?). Maybe they're interactive.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Let me try clicking on the black blocks at the top (timer)
# First, let me check their positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
black_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] == 4]
print("Black 4-pixel nodes (timer blocks):")
for n in black_nodes:
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try clicking on one of them
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 1, 'col': 11}])
print(f"\nClick on black block: board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- Row 17-18: Y (2x2), c (2x2), Y (2x2)
- Row 23-24: Y (2x2), c (2x4), Y (2x2)
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just try to understand the goal by looking at the initial state and the current state.
Actually, I just realized something. The game says the HUD is `rows 19-46, cols 0-51`. This is the area where the rotating blocks are. The game considers this area as the HUD because it changes predictably with each action.
But the game also says `gameplay_changed: False` when I click on the red or green shapes. This means the game doesn't consider the color rotation as a gameplay change.
So the game thinks the rotating blocks are the HUD, and clicking on the red/green shapes only changes the HUD, not the gameplay.
But the only interaction is rotating the colors. So either:
1. The goal is to rotate the colors to a specific configuration (but I've tried all 20 rotations)
2. The goal is something else entirely, and I haven't found the right interaction yet
Let me try clicking on the black blocks at the top (the timer?). Maybe they're interactive.
